content.problemStatement:
Chefland is a big city which consists of infinite number of junctions on a 2D plane. The junctions are depicted below and they form a hexagonal grid — they satisfy the following conditions:


Two of the junctions have coordinates (0,0)(0,0)(0,0) and (1,0)(1,0)(1,0).
Each junction JJJ is connected by line segments with six other junctions. These junctions are the vertices of a regular hexagon with side length 111 and their distances from JJJ are also all equal to 111.


Chef created a robot that can traverse the junctions. Initially, it is in some junction and oriented towards another junction which is connected to it by a line segment. The robot accepts a string of instructions — decimal digits 000 through 555. It executes the instructions one by one and stops after executing the final instruction. When the robot executes an instruction denoted by a digit ddd, it first rotates by 60⋅d60 \cdot d60⋅d degrees counterclockwise and then moves 111 unit forward, to the junction it is oriented towards.

Chef wants to test the robot, so he wrote a string SSS of NNN instructions. You should answer QQQ queries. In each query, Chef wants to give the robot a substring of instructions SL,SL+1,…,SRS_L, S_{L+1}, \ldots, S_RSL​,SL+1​,…,SR​; the robot starts in the junction (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) and it is oriented towards (1,0)(1, 0)(1,0). You should find the final position (the xxx- and yyy-coordinates) of the robot.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106
the sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ.
The second line contains a single string SSS with length NNN.
QQQ lines follow. Each of these lines contains two space-separated integers LLL and RRR describing a query.
content.outputFormat:
For each query, print a single line containing two space-separated real numbers — the coordinates of the robot. Your answer will be considered correct if the absolute or relative error of each coordinate does not exceed 10−610^{-6}10−6.

**Warning:** Outputting more than 8 digits after floating point may result in exceeding output size limit and receiving "Runtime error" verdict.
